beats.text: A Markov chain throws the history away: the next state depends only on the present state, and nothing earlier.

visual.sub: Memorylessness turns a sequence into a matrix

beats.text: Conditioning on the entire past collapses to conditioning on the single current state, giving the transition probability P sub i j.

visual.note: EQ S7.1 — the whole subject in one line

beats.text: Collect the probabilities into a transition matrix; one step of the chain is one matrix multiply of the distribution vector.
In the two-state weather model, sunny stays sunny with probability 0.7 and rainy stays rainy with probability 0.6.
Starting certainly sunny and iterating, the distribution marches toward a fixed point near 0.571 sunny regardless of the start.
Because next state reads only the current state, this chain is order one, and any order-k chain re-encodes as order one.
A distribution is stationary when applying the chain leaves it unchanged: one step in, the same distribution out.

visual.sub: A fixed point you can solve for

beats.text: The stationary distribution is the left eigenvector of P for eigenvalue one, normalized to sum to one.
An irreducible, aperiodic finite chain has a unique stationary distribution and converges to it from every start.
Detailed balance asks for as much probability flowing i to j as flows back, which is a stronger local condition that implies stationarity.
For the two-state chain, the stationary probability of A is 0.4 divided by 0.7, which equals four-sevenths.
PageRank is this engine: a damped random surfer with alpha near 0.15 has a stationary distribution that is its page rank.
A Hidden Markov Model runs a Markov chain underneath, but you never see the states, only noisy emissions.

visual.sub: Infer hidden weather from visible umbrellas

beats.text: The joint distribution stacks two assumptions: an initial term, transitions between hidden states, and emissions for each observation.
The forward recursion carries a belief vector, blending where you came from with whether the state explains what you saw.
Three canonical questions each have an exact dynamic program: Forward for likelihood, Viterbi for decoding, Baum-Welch for learning.
Summing over hidden paths looks hopeless, but dynamic programming brings the cost down to K squared times T.
The first forward value seeds the recursion: initial Rainy 0.6 times umbrella emission 0.3 gives 0.18.
MCMC inverts the problem: you are given a target distribution and you construct a chain whose stationary distribution is exactly it.
The defining pain of Bayesian inference is the normalizing constant, an intractable high-dimensional integral in the denominator.
The acceptance rule uses the target only as a ratio, so the unknown normalizing constant cancels entirely.
Uphill moves toward higher density are always taken; a downhill move from density two to one is accepted half the time.
Step size is the central knob, with a folklore target acceptance rate near 0.234 for high-dimensional random-walk Metropolis.
Over many steps the running histogram of accepted samples grows into the target, the time-average equalling the space-average.

concepts.title: Gibbs sampling & MCMC in modern ML

beats.text: Gibbs sampling exploits structure: sample each variable from its full conditional given all the others, cycling through coordinates.

visual.sub: Move axis by axis through the joint

beats.text: Each coordinate is drawn from its conditional with the others held at their freshest values, then the sweep moves on.
Gibbs is Metropolis-Hastings with acceptance probability always equal to one, since proposing from the exact conditional never rejects.
Modern probabilistic programming defaults to NUTS, an adaptive Hamiltonian sampler using gradients of log pi for long informed trajectories.
Diffusion image and video generators are learned reverse-time Markov chains, walking a noisy chain backward from noise to signal.
The honest caveat: convergence is asymptotic and unprovable in finite time, and effective sample sizes can be tiny.
